scenario: Willow Logistics maintains 3 microservices written in C# that integrate with Camunda 8.8 to handle 900 shipment tracking jobs per day. A team lead wants to align worker implementation on the same C# library that Writing good workers uses for its example so onboarding new engineers stays consistent.
question: Which C# library does the Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers page direct .NET teams to use for worker code?

options.text: The C# client at github.com/camunda/camunda-platform-get-started/tree/master/csharp is the library used in the Writing good workers example for .NET worker code on Camunda 8.8 and aligns with the get-started repository

A standalone NuGet package called Camunda.Zeebe.Client is positioned as the recommended C# library, while the client in the get-started repository is described as a learning artifact not suitable for production worker code

The Camunda 7 External Task Client for .NET is positioned as the C# library for Camunda 8.8 workers; the broker exposes a compatibility endpoint that accepts external task fetch and lock calls from 7.x clients

No C# library is recommended; Writing good workers tells .NET teams to consume the Camunda 8.8 REST API directly through HttpClient and to wrap their own thin client around it for every new worker project

a.text: Correct. The C# section of Writing good workers links to the camunda-platform-get-started/tree/master/csharp client as the C# example for writing worker code, anchoring .NET teams on the same library used in the get-started materials.

b.text: Incorrect. The page directly links to the get-started C# client without positioning it as unsuitable for production worker code.

c.text: Incorrect. The Camunda 7 External Task Client is not the C# library Writing good workers points to for Camunda 8.8.

d.text: Incorrect. The page does provide a C# example based on a published client rather than telling teams to wrap HttpClient themselves.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers best practice directs .NET teams to the C# client in the camunda-platform-get-started/csharp directory as the basis for worker implementations.
